content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 11, 2017

Molly L. Dillon
Assistant City Attorney
100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 8
Santa Rosa CA 95404

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-139

Dear Ms. Dillon:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090 and the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sonoma County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. In light of a councilmember's employment with the program administrator of the Property Assessed Clean Energy ("PACE") program for the Golden State Finance Authority, does Section 1090 or the Act preclude the City of Santa Rosa from joining the authority and authorizing the program administrator to operate within the City?

2. In light a of a councilmember's employment with the program administrator, does Section 1090 or the Act preclude the City from entering an agreement with the program administrator setting forth general compliance requirements for the PACE program and providing for the administrator's indemnification of the City?

3. If prohibited, would the answer change if the councilmember relinquished his stock options in the program administrator, while remaining an employee of the company, or if the councilmember resigned from the administrator, even if he retains his stock options?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-139
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

1. Section 1090 does not preclude the City of Santa Rosa from joining the authority and
authorizing the program administrator to operate within the City. However, the Act would require
the councilmember to recuse himself from the decisions.

2. Section 1090 does prohibit the City from entering into an agreement with the program
administrator setting forth general compliance requirements for the PACE Program and providing
for the administrator's indemnification of the City.

3. If the councilmember resigns and has options for shares totaling less than three percent of
the program administrator's shares, Section 1090 would not prohibit the City from entering into an
agreement with the administrator, although the councilmember would be required to recuse himself
from the decision under Section 1090 and the Act.

FACTS

You are the Assistant City Attorney for the City of Santa Rosa and write on behalf of
Councilmember Chris Rogers. Councilmember Rogers is employed in the position of Policy and
Legislative Affairs for Ygrene Energy Fund, Inc., a Delaware Corporation registered to do business
in California. He has been employed with Ygrene for just over one year. His current duties include
providing national policy guidance for states interested in adopting enabling legislation for the
PACE program, as well as analysis of, and direction on, future industry legislation and regulation.
He is not engaged in the expansion of Ygrene operations into additional local jurisdictions.

As part of Councilmember Roger's employment compensation, he has received employee
stock options, which are an accrual of options to purchase Ygrene stock for a set price when the
options vest. Councilmember Rogers' stock options are currently not vested, and while the exact
percentage of ownership represented by Councilmember Rogers' stock options is unknown, you
stated that they represent a very small interest in the company and certainly less than five percent.

Ygrene is the administrator of the PACE program for Golden State Finance Authority, a
Joint Powers Authority ("JPA"). PACE programs are local government programs (or JPA
sponsored programs) authorized by legislation to provide financing to property owners for energy,
water and renewable generation improvements to fixed property that is repaid through the property
tax system. For any PACE program to operate in a jurisdiction, the governing body of the
jurisdiction must pass a resolution allowing participation of resident property owners in the
program.

The City currently allows for participation by property owners within the City in only one
PACE program. That Program is sponsored by the County of Sonoma and is called the Sonoma
County Energy Independence Program. To provide other options and sources of funding, the
County of Sonoma has authorized other PACE programs to operate within the unincorporated areas
of the County. These PACE programs are all sponsored by JPAs and operated by third-party
program administrators. Program administrators for the programs authorized by the County

File No. A-17-139
Page No. 3

including Ygrene, have requested that the City authorize their operation of separate PACE
programs within the City.

In response to these requests, which occurred prior to Councilmember Rogers election to the
City Council, the Santa Rosa City Council will be considering whether to expand the number of
PACE programs allowed to operate within the City. With respect to each new PACE program, the
City Council will consider whether to (1) authorize the City to join the PACE program administrator's
sponsoring JPA (except with respect to California FIRST, where the City is already a member of
the JPA), (2) allow the PACE program administrator to operate within the City's boundaries
making PACE loans to property owners within the City, and (3) enter an agreement with each
newly authorized PACE program administrator setting forth general compliance requirements for
the program providing for the program administrator's indemnification of the City.

While separate agreements with each PACE program administrator are not required to
authorize a PACE program to operate within the City, City staff is recommending that the City
obtain a separate agreement with each program administrator in connection with any authorization
to operate its PACE program within the City. The foregoing actions do not include any exchange of
funds between the City and any new PACE program administrators. The City staff does not propose
charging the PACE program administrators, receiving any remuneration in connection with the
PACE program loans, or paying any remuneration to the program administrators for operating
within the City's boundaries.

Each authorized PACE program administrator will receive revenue by charging each
borrower within the City, including interest and fees associated with PACE program loans. Based
on the current staff proposal, the City does not anticipate performing any audits or active
monitoring of the PACE programs at this time, but it may respond to and investigate consumer
complaints, if any, as it may deem necessary and appropriate. It is possible that the City Council
will direct staff to provide additional options that include greater oversight or the collection of an
administrative fee from the PACE program administrators to cover the City's costs, but this is not
part of the current staff proposal.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making
contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests,
other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty
and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58
Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to
strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d
191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates

File No. A-17-139
Page No. 4

Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties.
(Id. at pp. 646-649.)

In this instance, Councilmember Rogers is a public officer subject to the provisions of
Section 1090. Moreover, because the City Council has the power to execute the City's contracts,
Councilmember Rodgers is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of all contracts by
the City Council irrespective of whether he actually participates in the making of the contract.
(Thomson v. Call, supra, at pp. 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del Norte
(1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).) Thus, the determinative questions are
whether (1) authorizing a PACE program to operate within the City or an agreement with a Pace
program administrator is a contract for purposes of Section 1090 and (2) whether Councilmember
Rogers has a financial interest in the contract.

Does the decision at issue involve a contract?

To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234
(1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Section 1090 and 1097 require
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of
'contract.'" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) However, in
interpreting the definition of contract for purposes of Section 1090, the Attorney General's office
has opined that a decision regarding a "license" or "regulatory permit" was a "regulatory matter and
not a 'contract' in the ordinary sense of the word." (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen., supra, at p. 36; see also
Miller Advice Letter, No. A-15-229.)

In this case, the City must authorize any Pace program that operates within the City. And
while not required, the City is proposing an agreement with each Pace program administrator. In
regard to authorizing a Pace program to operate within the City, decisions to join Golden State
Finance Authority and authorize it to operate through Ygrene, it's program administrator, within the
City are not decisions involving a contract because they are regulatory in nature. However, any
agreement with Ygrene as a program administrator is a contract and subject to Section 1090.

Does the official have a financial interest in the contract?

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333), and officials are deemed to have a financial
interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section 1090 nowhere
specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General Opinions state that
prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or
the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (See e.g., Thomson, supra, at pp.
645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5.)

As an employee of Ygrene, Councilmember Rodgers has a financial interest in any
agreement between the City and Ygrene.

File No. A-17-139
Page No. 5

Accordingly, Section 1090 prohibits the City from entering an agreement with Ygrene
regarding its duties as the program administer for Golden State Finance Authority. Section 1090
does not however prohibit the City from separately deciding to join the Golden State Finance
Authority and authorizing it to operate through Ygrene within the City, although Councilmember
Rogers must recuse himself from the decision as required under the Act and analyzed below.

Additionally, you have asked whether Section 1090 applies if Councilmember Rogers
resigns from Ygrene and/or relinquishes his stock options. In this regard, merely relinquishing the
stock options would not change the analysis because Councilmember Rodgers would continue to
have an interest in any agreement between the City an Ygrene as an employee of Ygrene. If
Councilmember Rogers merely resigns, the stock options will also constitute an interest in any
contract between the City and Ygrene, and thereby prohibit the City from entering an agreement
with Ygrene, unless the options are for shares totaling less than three percent of Ygrene's shares.
However, if Councilmember Rogers resigns and relinquishes his options, he has no interest in an
agreement between the City and Ygrene, and the City would not be prohibited from entering an
agreement with Ygrene although Councilmember Rogers must recuse himself from the decision as
required under the Act and analyzed below.

The Political Reform Act

We have determined that Section 1090 prohibits the City from entering an agreement with
Ygrene as the program administer for Golden State Finance Authority. However, the City is
prohibited from making separate decisions to join Golden State Finance Authority and authorize it
to operate within the City. Similarly, the City is not prohibited from decisions regarding an
agreement with Ygrene if Councilmember Rodgers resigns and holds options for shares of less than
three percent of Ygrene's shares. In these circumstances in which 1090 does not prohibit the action,
we must determine whether councilmember Rodgers is disqualified under the Act.

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from taking part in governmental decisions in which
they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial interest in
a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the official's interests specified in Section 87103.

As pertinent to this request, an official has an interest in any business entity in which the
official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) Additionally,
an official has an interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more received by, or
promised to, the official within the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) As a paid
employee of Ygrene, it appears that Councilmember Rodgers has interests in Ygrene as both a
business entity and a source of income.

¹ You have stated that the options represent less than five percent of Ygrene's shares but that the exact
percentage is unknown. If the options are for shares totaling less than three percent of Ygrene's shares and
Councilmember Rogers resigns, the City would not be prohibited from entering into an agreement with Ygrene under
Section 1090 because the shares would be either a remote interest under Section 1091(b)(14) or a noninterest under
Section 1091.5(a)(1).)

File No. A-17-139
Page No. 6

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Moreover, a financial effect on a business entity, including a business entity that is a source of income, is material if the entity is a named party in, or subject of the decision. (Regulations 18702.1(a) and 18702.3.)

In this case Ygrene is a named party, or subject of any decisions to join Golden State Finance Authority and authorize Ygrene to operate within the City as the program administrator, as well as any decisions to enter an agreement with Ygrene. Accordingly, the financial effect of these decisions on Councilmember Roger's interests in Ygrene is foreseeable and material. To the extent that Section 1090 does not preclude the City from making the decisions, Councilmember Rodgers must recuse himself from the decisions. In the event Councilmember Rodgers resigns from his position from Ygrene, he will have an interest in Ygrene under the Act for 12 months from the date on which he receives his last payment of income from Ygrene and must continue to recuse himself from any decisions with a foreseeable and material effect on Ygrene during this period. To the extent that he retains any stock options in Ygrene, Councilmember Rodgers must also continue to recuse himself if the options are worth $2,000 or more.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By:     Brian G. Lau
        Senior Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. In light of a councilmember's employment with the program administrator of the Property 
Assessed Clean Energy ("PACE") program for the Golden State Finance Authority, does Section 
1090 or the Act-preclude the City of Santa Rosa from joining the authority and authorizing the 
program administrator to operate within the City? 
2. In light a of a councilmember's employment with the program administrator, does Section 
I 090 or the Act preclude the City from entering an agreement with the program administrator 
setting forth general compliance requirements for the PACE program and providing for the 
administrator's indemnification bf the City? 
3. If prohibited, would the answer change if the councilmember relinquished his stock 
options in the program administrator, while remaining an employee of the company, or if the 
councilmember resigned from the administrator, even ifhe retains his stock options?

CONCLUSION: 1. Section 1090 does not preclude the City of Santa Rosa from joining the authority and 
authorizing the program administrator to operate within the City. However, the Act would require 
the councilmember to recuse himself from the decisions. 
2. Section 1090 does prohibit the City from entering into an agreement with the program 
adminislralor setting forlh general wmpliance requirements for the PACE Program and providing 
for the administrator's indemnification of the City. 
3. If the councilmember resigns and has options for shares totaling less than three percent of 
the program administrator's shares, Section 1090 would not prohibit the City from entering into an 
agreement with the administrator, although the councilmember would be required to recuse himself 
from the decision under Section 1090 and the Act.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 11, 2017 Molly L. Dillon Assistant City Attorney 100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 8 Santa Rosa CA 95404 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-17-139 Dear Ms. Dillon: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090 and the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Sonoma County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTIONS 1. In light of a councilmember's employment with the program administrator of the Property Assessed Clean Energy ("PACE") program for the Golden State Finance Authority, does Section 1090 or the Act preclude the City of Santa Rosa from joining the authority and authorizing the program administrator to operate within the City? 2. In light a of a councilmember's employment with the program administrator, does Section 1090 or the Act preclude the City from entering an agreement with the program administrator setting forth general compliance requirements for the PACE program and providing for the administrator's indemnification of the City? 3. If prohibited, would the answer change if the councilmember relinquished his stock options in the program administrator, while remaining an employee of the company, or if the councilmember resigned from the administrator, even if he retains his stock options? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-139 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION 1. Section 1090 does not preclude the City of Santa Rosa from joining the authority and authorizing the program administrator to operate within the City. However, the Act would require the councilmember to recuse himself from the decisions. 2. Section 1090 does prohibit the City from entering into an agreement with the program administrator setting forth general compliance requirements for the PACE Program and providing for the administrator's indemnification
